Tafseer:
1. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(By the Mount) (1) - 'Wa' (By) is for an oath, and this has been explained in detail in its relevant place. (Footnote 1) In the Qur'an, oaths are mostly sworn to serve as testimony for the claim mentioned after the oath as the subject of the oath (muqsam 'alayh). Here, the subject of the oath, as we noted in the introduction, is: (Indeed, the punishment of your Lord is to occur) (verse 7)—meaning: indeed, the punishment of your Lord is bound to occur. Therefore, the oath by the Mount is sworn to serve as testimony for this claim, and keeping this in view, we have translated it as: 'By the Mount'. This translation is highly meaningful. 'Tur' refers to Mount Tur (Sinai), which is mentioned repeatedly in the Qur'an from various perspectives. It was in its sacred valley of Tuwa that Moses (peace be upon him), while returning from Madyan, witnessed the first manifestation of God. There, he was honored with messengership, and commanded to go to Pharaoh and his people as a warner to warn them that if they did not desist from their transgression, they would face His punishment. After Pharaoh was destroyed, Allah Almighty called Moses to this very Mount to receive the Torah. During Moses' absence, when the Children of Israel fell into the fitnah of calf-worship, Moses brought the leaders of his people to the foot of this Mount to pray for collective repentance. On that occasion, Allah Almighty shook the entire Mount and warned the Children of Israel: He was taking a covenant from them through His book, the Torah, and if they did not stand firm on this covenant, His power could crush them through this very mountain.
Due to these various characteristics, Mount Tur is a great historical monument testifying to the truth of the claim mentioned after the oath. For this very reason, an oath is sworn by it in Surah At-Tin, where, as we will clarify in its commentary, (Footnote 2) it is also sworn to prove the Recompense.
Clearing a misunderstanding: Some have taken the word 'tur' here to mean mountains in general, but this opinion is weak for several reasons. First, the Qur'an should be interpreted in accordance with the recognized meaning. While 'tur' can mean a mountain in Hebrew or Syriac, it is not commonly used in this sense in Arabic. In the Qur'an, this word appears repeatedly, and always as a proper noun. Second, taking it as mountains in general would lose its harmony with the subject of the oath, whereas harmony is necessary lest the oath becomes out of place.
_____
Footnote 1: [This footnote likely refers to the commentary of related verses mentioned in the original text.]
Footnote 2: [This footnote likely refers to the commentary of related verses mentioned in the original text.]

By 'a Register written' (wa kitabin-mastoor) is meant the Torah. A clear indication for this is that it is connected to the Mount, and Allah Almighty bestowed this Book upon Moses (peace be upon him) on that very Mount. A second indication is its subsequent attribute: 'In parchment spread open' (fi raqqim-manshoor). 'Raqq' refers to fine parchment made of animal skin, which was used for writing in ancient times, and history shows that initially, the Torah was written on refined fine parchment, which was rolled into scrolls and unrolled for recitation. With these indications, there is no room to interpret this as any book other than the Torah. Some have interpreted this as the record of deeds, but this is not its place; its testimony is effective in the Hereafter, not in this world.
Its connection with the subject of the oath is also entirely clear. Just as the Mount is a historical monument of Allah's law of retribution, the Torah is a great written record in which His law of retribution is documented, along with the history of how He dealt with nations under this law. Reading this history, it is clear that the law of retribution has been active since Adam (peace be upon him), and this continuity is a proof that it will continue, until a Day comes when His perfect justice manifests. On that Day, whoever does an atom's weight of evil will face its punishment, and whoever does an atom's weight of good will receive its reward.
The indefinite form of 'kitab' (Register) is to highlight its grandeur. This is because before the Qur'an, this was the primary Book Allah Almighty revealed to guide and warn of His punishment, and He gave its fundamental commands to Moses (peace be upon him) in writing. The teachings of past prophets (peace be upon them) were oral; the written form of revelation began with this Book, which is why it is mentioned with special care. We will discuss the benefits of this written revelation in our commentary on Surah Ar-Rahman and Surah Al-Alaq.

'In parchment spread open' (fi raqqim-manshoor) - 'Raqq' was explained above. Referencing that this Book was in open sheets is to establish the proof—meaning: it is not a hidden matter, but an open reality written in open and spread pages, which anyone can read, or have read to them. Indeed, they have the right to demand of its bearers to tell and recite to them what Allah Almighty has explained in it. Note that the Arabs recognized the religious superiority of the People of the Book, and the Qur'an repeatedly directed them to ask the People of the Book if they did not know. Furthermore, the Torah also documented the history of Abraham and Ishmael (peace be upon them), whom the Arabs claimed as their ancestors and whose religion they claimed to inherit. Thus, it was a subtle indication that they could learn from this Book whether their ancestors warned them of the Recompense or not.
A subtle rebuke to the Jews of the time: The word 'manshoor' (spread open) contains a subtle rebuke to the Jews of the time. The Qur'an states in various places that they concealed parts of their scriptures. They chose this concealment of truth during their period of decline, primarily because they did not want people to know of the prophecies in the Torah regarding the final Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), which warned them that if they rejected him, they would face His wrath and lose their leadership. Through this word, they are reminded of the actual nature of the Torah—namely, it was revealed in open, clear pages to be read and understood, but they chose to conceal it. Jesus pointed to this: they were given a lamp to place on a stand to give light to the house, but they hid it under a bowl.

The meaning of 'Al-Bayt-ul-Ma'mur':
Commentators have generally interpreted 'Al-Bayt-ul-Ma'mur' (the frequented House) as a house in Paradise that holds the same status for the angels in heaven as the Kaaba holds for humans on the earth. However, in our view, this is not relevant here. What value can the testimony of such a house in Paradise have for the claim presented after the oaths? Since commentators have the misconception that the entity sworn by must be sacred, they looked only at the sanctity of the object, instead of its relationship with the claim. In this connection, no relationship is found.
Some have interpreted this as the Kaaba. This opinion is valuable because the secure city (Mecca) was sworn by in Surah At-Tin to prove the Recompense. Since the Kaaba is located in that secure city, its testimony is meaningful; but the context is contrary to this opinion. This is because the subsequent oath is by 'the canopy raised high' (as-saqf-il-marfoo'), which obviously cannot refer to the roof of the Kaaba, but to the heaven. If it refers to the heaven, then swearing by the Kaaba, followed by the heaven, and then the sea, becomes disconnected. It lacks the harmony present between the Mount and the written Register.
In our view, 'Al-Bayt-ul-Ma'mur' refers to this earth, over which the canopy of the heaven is spread. Several arguments support this view:
First, the metaphor of a house (bayt) is highly appropriate for the earth. The Qur'an repeatedly describes it as a resting place (mihad) and a cradle (qirar). It also presents the metaphor of the earth as a floor and the heaven as its roof.
Second, the subsequent mention of the heaven is a clear indication that this refers to the earth. In the Qur'an, where the signs of His power are mentioned, the heavens and earth are mentioned together.
Third, the Qur'an repeatedly explains that the arrangement of providence He made in this earth for human sustenance is a proof that man is not an unbridled beast, but is accountable before his Lord. 'Ma'mur' (frequented/populated) points to these resources of His providence, and to the requirement of accountability.
Fourth, in the previous Surah, He stated: (And on the earth are signs for those of certain faith) (verse 20), and: (And in the heaven is your provision and whatever you are promised) (verse 22). In our commentary on those verses, we explained how the signs of the heavens and earth testify to His law of retribution and His punishment. This same is presented here, with only a difference in style. For these reasons, 'Al-Bayt-ul-Ma'mur' refers to the earth, and describing it by this attribute points to His power, wisdom, and providence, which argue for His justice and recompense, and which we repeatedly explain in this book.

The testimony of the heaven:
After the earth, the testimony of the heaven is presented. The Qur'an generally references the signs of the heaven alongside those of the earth. In the previous Surah, He stated: (And in the heaven is your provision and whatever you are promised) (51:22), and: (By the heaven full of pathways) (51:7). Refer to our commentary on those verses, showing how His winds' actions in the heaven played a primary role in the destruction of the transgressors. The heaven is mentioned in the Qur'an with various attributes in accordance with the context. Here, it is described as 'the canopy raised high' (as-saqf-il-marfoo'). 'Saqf' (canopy/roof) is a proof of His providence and mercy—showing His generosity in spreading this magnificent canopy over our heads; and 'marfoo'' (raised high) manifests His power and majesty—showing that the Being Who can raise this vast canopy can easily perform any task. The various ways in which these attributes of His mercy and power argue for the Hereafter are repeatedly explained in this book.

The meaning of 'sajr' is filling. 'Sajara-r-rajulu-t-tannur' means: he filled the oven with fuel. 'Sajara-l-ma'u-n-nahr' means: water filled the river.
The testimony of the sea:
After the earth and heaven, He swore by the greatest sign of the earth—the sea. Note that in other places of the Qur'an, the signs of the sea are mentioned after those of the heavens and earth—refer to Surah Ar-Rahman, verses 10-24. In those verses, you will see that among the signs of the earth, the sea contains countless testimonies to His monotheism, power, providence, and recompense. We repeatedly explain these testimonies. Some important aspects will also be discussed under Surah Ar-Rahman. Here, keeping in view the context of the Surah and the subject of the oaths, note that the punishment of which they are warned is supported by the historical accounts of Noah's flood and Pharaoh's drowning, both of whom were destroyed by the sea.

The core claim which is the theme of the Surah:
This is the core claim proved by the oaths, serving as the theme of the Surah. This same was stated in the previous Surah after the oath by the winds as: (Indeed, what you are promised is true, and indeed, the Recompense is to occur) (51:5-6). The only difference is that the aspect of punishment is prominent here. We explained under that verse that it refers to the punishment of which the messengers warned in this world and the Hereafter. In this Surah, what was implied there is made explicit. Stating the detail after the summary and making explicit what was implied is a recognized style in the Qur'an.
We pointed to the testimonies that serve as proofs of this claim. Reflecting on them, He addressed the Prophet, saying: the punishment of which you warn the Quraysh is bound to occur, and no one will be able to prevent it—meaning neither their power nor their assumed intercessors will save them from it; no one will save them in this world or the Hereafter. The revelation Moses received on Mount Tur, the written record of the Torah, the signs of this earth, the majestic canopy of the heaven, and the lessons of this sea are sufficient to confirm this warning. Those whose eyes are not opened by these signs will not believe even if they see a part of the heaven falling on them, but will regard it as a rain cloud, until it destroys them; they will never believe.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 7 and 8 was combined in the original text.]

Depiction of the Day of punishment:
This portrays the upheaval of that Day: in this world, this heaven, which appears solid and firm, will sway. 'Mawr' means moving in panic in all directions—meaning one part of it will go in one direction, and another in another direction; and these mountains, which appear firmly fixed, will be removed and set in motion. The meaning is: on that Day, when the heaven and mountains face this end, what will befall human beings? Will their power, fortresses, or strongholds benefit them on that Day! Note that when the Quraysh mocked the Resurrection, they would ask: 'When the Resurrection comes, what will happen to these mountains? Will they also be devastated?' Answering them, He stated: tell them that my Lord will reduce them to dust. (Then woe, that Day, to the deniers) - He stated: on that Day, destruction is for those who are denying it today. (Those who are in [empty] discourse playing) (verse 12) - this describes the deniers, showing that the discussion is about those rebellious Quraysh who fabricated various objections to mock the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and devalue his warning. These objections will be detailed under verses 29-46.
The opponents' empty discourse:
'Khawd' means entering into something. 'Khadal-ma'u' means: he entered the water. From this is derived 'khada-l-qawmu fi-l-hadith', which means people went from one point of discussion to another. Generally, this idiom is used for fabricating arguments to defend falsehood and oppose the truth. It is used in this sense in various places—for example: (and you engaged [in empty discourse] like that in which they engaged) (At-Tawbah, 9:69), and in Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 83: (So leave them to converse and play until they meet their Day which they are promised). Trying to fabricate arguments against what one knows is true is a form of play with your own intellect; hence, the Qur'an described it as play (la'ib). This play is extremely dangerous when its consequence is eternal loss. This discourse will be detailed, pointing to the statements they made against their own conscience to neutralize the impact of the Qur'an and the Prophet's words—such as calling him a soothsayer, a poet, a madman, or a fabricator, and claiming they could produce a similar discourse. This was a joke to them, and they did not realize they were preparing their own path to Hell.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 9 and 10 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 9, 10, and 11 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 9, 10, 11, and 12 was combined in the original text.]

The end of those who mock the Hereafter:
This details the calamities they will face on that Day. 'Da' ' means to push with force, and 'da'an' is added for emphasis—meaning: today they are busy in their play, but on that Day, they will be pushed into Hell with a violent push, and told: 'This is the Fire which you used to deny in the world!'
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 13 and 14 was combined in the original text.]

That is: in the world, when Allah's Messenger warned you of this, you tried to keep yourselves and your people untroubled by calling the Qur'an's style magic. Now tell Me: is this magic, or is it a reality, but you are still blind to it just as you were in the world!

Command will be given: 'Go, enter it; no escape is there for you. Whether you patiently endure or weep, both are equal for you—neither will your patience be rewarded, nor your wailing answered.' (You are only recompensed for what you used to do) - What you are facing is your own deeds. If any injustice had been committed against you, you could have expected your complaints to be answered; but when your own earnings are before you, against whom will you complain, and from whom will you expect help! (You are only recompensed for what you used to do) does not mean that their exact deeds will be laid before them, but their realities. In this world, whatever deed a person performs—good or bad—assumes a specific form before Allah in accordance with its effects, and in this form it will face him in the Hereafter; and though its shape will be different from that in the world, since it will be the harvest of what he sowed, He stated: you are receiving what you did.

To highlight the severity of the deniers' end, the reward of the righteous (muttaqeen) is mentioned. The contrast shows that the righteous are those who, instead of mocking, listened to the Prophet's warning with attention, reflected on it, and cherished it. It is stated: indeed, they will be in gardens and pleasure.

This details their enjoyment of these gardens and blessings. It is stated: they will enjoy whatever their Lord has given them with complete freedom. The ambiguity in 'what their Lord has given them' (bima atahum rabbuhum) highlights His majesty—showing that no one can estimate what He will bless them with; its reality will be known only to those who receive it. (And their Lord protected them from the punishment of Hellfire) is connected in my view to: (bima atahum rabbuhum)—meaning: they will enjoy His blessings, and will be specifically pleased with His favor that He protected them from the punishment of Hell. 'Fakiheen' (joyful) contains the meaning of being pleased and grateful; hence, no separate word was needed. This shows that the Hell which the deniers mock today will be regarded by the people of Paradise as their greatest protection, for which they will remain grateful. This theme appeared further on as: (They will say, 'Indeed, we were previously among our people fearful of punishment. So Allah conferred favor upon us and protected us from the punishment of the Scorching Wind') (verses 26-27).

They will be told: 'Eat and drink with satisfaction for what you used to do.' This eating and drinking will be wholesome, free from any harm or decrease, and without requiring any labor. 'Hani'an' is an adjective meaning wholesome. It is grammatically the attribute of an omitted verbal noun: (kulu wa-shrabu aklan wa-shurban hani'an). Some have taken it as a circumstantial clause (hal), but this is contrary to Arabic grammar.

They will enjoy His blessings, reclining on couches lined up. Couches lined up (sururin-masfoofah) is a description of the elegant arrangement of their sitting places, and also points to their mutual affection—meaning they will sit facing one another. Elsewhere, this is expressed as 'facing one another'. (And We will marry them to fair companions with large, [beautiful] eyes) - A person's joy is not complete without his family; therefore, Allah will arrange this blessing in Paradise. He has described them in words from which we can establish a basic concept, but the actual reality is known only to Allah, and will be visible in the Hereafter.

A glad tiding to complete the joy of the believers:
To complete their joy in Paradise, He gave this glad tiding: those of their descendants who followed them in faith, We will join them with them, even if the descendants did not deserve that high rank on their own. The rule is: (And We will not deprive them of anything of their deeds)—meaning: the standard of the descendants' faith will be completed without decreasing anything of the deeds of their ancestors. They will remain in the high ranks they deserved, and Allah Almighty, out of His special favor, will elevate the rank of their descendants. The restriction 'in faith' (bi-eemanin) in (And those who believed and whose descendants followed them in faith) shows that this favor is reserved only for those descendants who followed them in faith; if they lacked faith, they would not be worthy of this, even if they claimed to follow them. The indefinite form of 'eeman' (faith) shows that it has levels; if the descendants possessed even the lowest level of faith that made them worthy of Paradise, they would be eligible for this favor.
The core rule of salvation: (Every person, for what he earned, is retained) - This states the core rule: every person is bound to his own deeds. His deeds will save him or ruin him. It will not happen that without faith and righteous deeds, someone enters Paradise based on lineage. Allah Almighty will show His favor only to those who make themselves worthy of it through faith and deeds. This is a warning to both the Jews and the polytheists of the Arabs: they completely corrupted the religion of Abraham and Ishmael, but expected to receive high ranks in Paradise based on lineage. It may be asked: if the rule is that every person is bound to his own deeds, how can the descendants be elevated to the ranks of their ancestors? The answer is: this is a matter of Allah's special favor, which He has reserved for His believing servants. This does not negate the rule: faith and deeds are necessary for salvation, but this does not prevent Allah from elevating the ranks of His believing servants out of His favor. The two have different domains, and there is no contradiction between them.
Another deduction: This also reveals that just as He will gather the descendants with their ancestors to complete the joy of the ancestors—even if the descendants did not deserve that rank on their own—He will also gather the believing ancestors with their descendants to complete the joy of the descendants—even if the ancestors did not deserve that rank on their own. This is because the relationship of love is mutual.
The context of this glad tiding: If a person possesses true faith, his greatest anxiety regarding his children is that Allah Almighty should protect them from the terror of the Resurrection and the punishment of Hell. Worldly success holds a secondary status, and the prophets and the righteous prayed primarily for their children's success in the Hereafter. This was the case with those who accepted Islam in the Prophet's era. When their hearts were illuminated with faith, their greatest anxiety was not their children's worldly future, but their end in the Hereafter. This anxiety is mentioned in verse 26 of this Surah: (Indeed, we were previously among our people fearful of punishment). Under that verse, we will explain that this refers to their anxiety that no member of the family they were appointed as guardians over should fall into Hell. To reassure His believing servants, He gave this glad tiding: although salvation depends on faith and deeds, Allah Almighty will show His favor—if their descendants followed them in faith, even if the descendants' faith was of a lower degree, He will gather them with their ancestors, and for this gathering, He will not lower the ancestors' rank, but will elevate the descendants' rank.
Instruction to remain anxious for children's reform: This instructs everyone to make every effort to guide their children and relatives to the path of faith. Without faith, no one can achieve salvation, even if he is the offspring of a prophet. However, if the descendants chose the path of faith—even if their faith was of a lower degree—they will be joined with their noble ancestors in Paradise.

(And We will provide them with fruit and meat from whatever they desire) (22) - Just as We will gather their descendants, We will provide them with fruit and meat in abundance. 'We will provide' (amdadnahum) points to the reality that along with the increase in numbers, He will increase their provision and favor. 'Fruit' and 'meat' encompass all dietary blessings of Paradise.

(They will pass to one another therein a cup [of wine] wherein there is no ill speech or commission of sin) (23)
The joy and pleasure of Paradise:
This describes the joy of Paradise: they will pass to one another a cup of wine. 'Yatanaza'oona-l-ka's' means: they will pass the cup to one another, and snatching is not implied. 'Ka's' refers to both the cup and the wine. 'Wherein there is no ill speech or commission of sin' (la laghwun feeha wa-la ta'theem) - This wine will be free from the bad effects associated with worldly wine. It will not cause vain talk or slandering. 'Aththama' means slandering. Worldly wine makes people slander their rivals and their women in their intoxication, which caused long-lasting enmities in Jahiliyyah. The Qur'an pointed to this fitnah—showing that through it, Satan wants to ignite enmity and hatred among you. In contrast, the wine of Paradise will promote affection, as stated in Surah Al-Waqi'ah:
(They will not hear therein any ill speech or commission of sin, only a saying of 'Peace, peace') (Al-Waqi'ah, 56:25-26).

(And there will circulate among them young servants of theirs as if they were pearls well-protected) (24) - That is: young servants will be present before them to serve them, who, in their purity and beauty, will be like pearls protected in shells, brought out specifically for their service. The Arabs used 'pearls well-protected' (lu'lu'um-maknoon) to express the utmost beauty and purity. The Qur'anic description suggests that Allah Almighty will create these young servants specifically for this purpose. Some have argued otherwise, but they have no proof.

(And they will approach one another, questioning) (25)
The people of Paradise questioning one another:
Having gathered in Paradise, just as travelers who have covered a long and difficult journey question one another upon reaching their destination, the people of Paradise will turn to one another and ask: 'How did it go? What difficulties did you face? How did you reach the destination, and how was this gathering of family blessed?'

(They will say, 'Indeed, we were previously among our people fearful of punishment') (26) - They will respond: 'We lived among our families in fear of our Lord's punishment, anxious for their Hereafter alongside our own. We never grew arrogant of our wealth, family, or status, assuming they were our birthright. We kept the Hereafter foremost and prayed to our Lord to raise us as leaders of the righteous, not the wicked; we instructed our children to pray, give zakat, fear God, and die upon the true religion.'

(So Allah conferred favor upon us and protected us from the punishment of the Scorching Wind) (27) - 'Because of our fear and anxiety, Allah conferred His favor upon us, gathering us with our children in this Paradise and protecting us from the scorching wind of Hell.' 'Samoom' means the hot wind and heat of the Fire. This is expressed in a manner showing His great favor—meaning He protected them not only from the Fire, but even from its hot wind.

(Indeed, we used to supplicate Him before. Indeed, He is the Beneficent, the Merciful) (28)
Monotheism is the primary factor in salvation:
This is connected to: 'Indeed, we were previously among our people fearful of punishment'—meaning: we were anxious for our families, and we called upon Him alone in fear and hope, without ever associating anyone with Him. This should grammatically follow that phrase, but to highlight Allah's favor first, He stated: 'So Allah conferred favor upon us...' and postponed this. This highlights that monotheism is the primary factor in salvation. Whoever preserved it deserved His favor, and whoever corrupted it destroyed his Hereafter. The meaning is: we stood firm on this belief; therefore, Allah conferred His favor upon us.
'Indeed, He is the Beneficent, the Merciful' (innahu huwa-l-barru-r-raheem) - They will state this in deep gratitude: He is indeed faithful to His promise and Merciful—showing He not only fulfilled His promises, but blessed them with His further favors. 'Barr' means faithful. When used for Allah, it means He is faithful in fulfilling His promises. Truthfulness and loyalty are its core. Mentioning 'Merciful' alongside it shows that He not only fulfills His promises, but overlooks His servants' shortcomings to bless them with His favors.

2. Subject Matter of the Subsequent Verses (29-49)
Next, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is encouraged: continue to remind people through this Qur'an, ignoring their objections. Those who reject you will face their own ruin. It also answers the objections they raised against the Prophet—which were referred to as 'empty discourse' above. Their planning is described as a mere plot, and He reassured: they cannot harm the call to truth, but their plot will turn against them. Finally, he is reassured: no miracle can convince them; they will believe only when the Resurrection stands before them and their helplessness is clear; so be patient for the decision of your Lord, and glorify His praise to attain patience. Read the verses.
3. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(So remind, [O Muhammad], for you are not, by the favor of your Lord, a soothsayer or madman) (29) - Encouraging the Prophet:
The conjunction 'wa' (so/and) shows that what is stated here is metathetical to what was explained above. You saw that the truth of the Qur'an's warning was proved. Following this, addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) for comfort, He stated: continue to remind. Let the opponents mock; you are not, by the favor of your Lord, a soothsayer or madman. (by the favor of your Lord) (bi-ni'mati rabbika) is the proof here—meaning: the favor of your Lord upon you is so obvious to those who possess intellect that no one can deny it. What comparison can there be between the spring of wisdom flowing from your tongue and the nonsense of soothsayers! Which soothsayer ever possessed even a trace of your character? You are the perfect model of truthfulness, whereas soothsayers are liars. You receive revelation through the Holy Spirit, while soothsayers listen to the devils. Similarly, those who call you mad are mad themselves, for who have they found wiser and more rational than you! This accusation was contrary to their own knowledge; they used it only to deceive the common people. We discussed this in Surah Ash-Shu'ara.

(Or do they say, '[He is] a poet for whom we await a calamity of time'? Say, 'Wait, for indeed I am with you among those who wait') (30-31)
Answering another accusation of the opponents:
This refers to their second accusation. It is stated: do they call you a poet, expecting that like other poets, you will also perish, and your mission will vanish with time? It is commanded: tell them: 'Wait, for indeed I am with you among those who wait'—showing whether the warning I communicate to you is fulfilled, or the dream you are dreaming proves true. They called the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) a poet to neutralize the impact of the Qur'an's eloquence and majesty on people's hearts. They wanted to convince their followers: 'The impact within this speech is like that of our great poets; so do not fear any punishment, and do not despair of your ancestral religion. Like other poets, this person will also perish with time.' Before the migration, they told their people that the Helpers' support would not be blessed, and after the migration, they claimed his mission would end in a foreign environment. But the Qur'an's prediction proved true: the enemies' root was cut, and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was blessed with success.
(Say, 'Wait, for indeed I am with you among those who wait') - It is commanded: tell them: 'Wait, and I also am waiting whether your vain hope is realized, or the punishment of which your Lord has warned you manifests.' 'Manun' means time, and 'rayb-ul-manun' means the calamities of time. The Qur'an refuted this in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, presenting clear criteria for everyone to distinguish between a poet and a prophet. We discussed this there.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 30 and 31 was combined in the original text.]

(Or do their minds command them to this, or are they a transgressing people?) (32)
The primary cause of their opposition is rebelliousness:
That is: does their intellect command them to claim that you are a soothsayer, a madman, or a poet, or is it that they are a transgressing people who refuse to accept your words? The first is impossible: how can a rational person call someone of your truthfulness, composure, wisdom, and character a soothsayer, madman, or poet! If anyone does, it means he lacks intellect. Thus, only the second is true—namely: they are a transgressing people, and their rebelliousness is what makes them utter these statements; intellect has no share in this.

(Or do they say, 'He has fabricated it'? Rather, they do not believe. Then let them produce a statement like it, if they should be truthful) (33-34)
Answering another accusation:
This refers to their other accusation. It is stated: do they claim that he has fabricated this Qur'an on his own, but asserts to us that He revealed it through His angel? 'Taqawwal' means fabricating a lie, described elsewhere as: (He has fabricated it) (if-tarahu)—meaning: they claim it is his own fabrication, but he claims it is revealed by Allah to deceive people. (Rather, they do not believe) - This is the actual reality: this accusation is contrary to their own conscience. They know well that this is not your fabrication, and neither you nor anyone else can produce such a speech; but they do not want to believe, and therefore hide behind this accusation. (Then let them produce a statement like it...) - This is the challenge: if they truly believe it is his fabrication, let them produce a similar speech from their classical literature or gather together to produce one now. If they cannot (and they never can), they must withdraw this accusation and accept the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). This challenge remains active today. The deniers have never been able to produce a similar speech, and no such speech has appeared in history. Non-Arab nations can also try. This challenge is for the Qur'an as a whole, as a book of guidance. While a nation might claim to have a great poet, this is subjective, and cannot prove his prophethood; but the Qur'an's challenge is comprehensive.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 33 and 34 was combined in the original text.]

(Were they created by nothing, or were they the creators [of themselves]? Or did they create the heavens and the earth? Rather, they are not certain) (35-36)
Questions to the opponents:
Having answered their objections, He asks them: if they deny the Resurrection and recompense, were they created by nothing, or were they the creators of themselves? Or did they create the heavens and earth? Note that they believed Allah is the Creator of all. This question asks them: when you believe Allah is the Creator, why do you regard His power to resurrect you as impossible? Do you assume He is unable to recreate you? If He created you the first time, He can easily do so the second time. Furthermore, since you did not create yourselves, but He created you, is it possible that He should leave you unbridled, without questioning you? Similarly, since you did not create the heavens and earth, on what basis do you feel secure from the Creator's grasp? He can shake the earth to destroy you, or send a storm from the heaven to wipe you out in the twinkling of an eye.
The primary cause of denial:
'Rather, they are not certain' (bal la yooqinoon) - This reveals the cause: they accept all the premises that require the Resurrection, but they do not believe they are deserving of punishment and will face His grasp. Here, the object of 'yooqinoon' is omitted based on context.
We explain that they did not deny the Resurrection completely, but regarded it as unlikely, assuming their partners would save them. This made the Resurrection a meaningless thing to them. To refute this, He mentioned monotheism alongside the Resurrection.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 35 and 36 was combined in the original text.]

(Or have they the depositories of your Lord, or are they the controllers [of them]?) (37)
Another question:
This is of the same nature as the preceding questions. The meaning is: if they did not create themselves or the heavens and earth, do they possess the depositories of your Lord's favor, or are they appointed over them, that they feel secure from His grasp? That is: since they do not possess His depositories, why do they assume they will remain in ease and no one can deprive them? Allah can easily destroy their ease and bless those whom they regard as unworthy. This deals a blow to their pride, as they questioned why Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was chosen as a messenger instead of some wealthy leader. This also appeared in Surah Al-Qalam, verses 37-40.

(Or have they a stairway [into heaven] by which they listen? Then let their listener produce a clear authority) (38)
Baseless assumptions of the disbelievers:
That is: if they assume they will remain in ease in this world and the Hereafter, on what basis is this assumption? Do they have a stairway to heaven to listen to His decrees? Has a book been revealed to them in which their desires are recorded? Has Allah given them a certificate of safety? If their heavenly informant has told them this, let them produce a clear proof. This is stated briefly here, and in detail in Surah Al-Qalam, verses 37-40. Based on grammar, some words are omitted here, which are unpacked in the translation. This also shows that man is dependent on heavenly guidance; so since everyone cannot receive it, they are obligated to follow those who receive it.

(Or has He daughters while you have sons?) (39)
Their assumed intercessors are baseless:
That is: is your trust based on your assumed intercessors? If so, the foolishness of this is demonstrated by the fact that you have attributed daughters to God, whereas you prefer sons for yourselves and loathe daughters. The meaning is: if you did not reflect on whether He has partners, you should at least have avoided attributing to Him what you dislike for yourselves. But you committed a double foolishness: associating partners with Him, and associating what you dislike.
The polytheists believed three goddesses—Allat, Al-Uzza, and Manat—were God's daughters who could intercede for them, described in the next Surah as:
(Have you considered Al-Lat and Al-'Uzza, and Manat, the third—the other? Is for you the male and for Him the female? That, then, is an unjust division. They are not but [mere] names you have named, you and your forefathers, for which Allah has sent down no authority) (An-Najm, 53:19-23).

(Or do you ask them a payment, so they are burdened by debt?) (40)
Amazement at their aversion:
This expresses amazement at their aversion: why do they not listen to you? You are not asking them for any payment for communicating the message, such that they are burdened by it? You are sharing His blessing freely, so what prevents them from listening? Note that knowledge is a beloved thing, and no reasonable person should refuse to listen to what he does not know. Such a person is like a blind man who is stumbling but rejects the hand that wants to guide him. The Arabs were unlettered, and should have eagerly sought His guidance—especially since they knew from Ishmael's traditions that a messenger would be raised among them to bring them out of darkness into light. They should have listened to him, but they chose the opposite, at which the Qur'an expresses amazement.

(Or do they have [knowledge of] the unseen, so they write [it] down?) (41)
That is: since they are so averse to your guidance, do they possess the knowledge of the unseen, which they are writing down, asserting that they have high ranks in the Hereafter? Obviously, they have no such means. They should have hastened to your call, as you receive revelation from Allah; but they are averse to you, and have fabricated their own Shariah from their desires, assuming it is His religion. This was described in Surah An-Najm, verse 35: (Does he have knowledge of the unseen, so he sees?)—pointing to those who made no preparation but assumed they would receive high ranks based on lineage, mocking the Prophet and waiting for some calamity to destroy him.

(Or do they intend a plan? But those who disbelieve—they are the ones [caught] in a plan) (42)
Exposing the actual reality:
This question exposes the actual reality: their objections are merely a cover. The actual cause is that they see their leadership threatened by the call to truth; therefore, they fabricate these objections to prevent their people from accepting the call. It is stated: do they intend a plan against you? If so, those who disbelieve are the ones caught in their own plan.
Those who plot against the truth are caught in their own plan:
The meaning is: those who plot against the truth and try to turn people away from it do not harm the callers, but destroy themselves. The only path of success is the one shown by Allah; whoever deviates to follow his desires and fabricates objections will realize at the end that he was caught in his own plan.

(Or do they have a deity other than Allah? Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him) (43)
Shirk has no connection with God:
Since everyone must return to Allah for the account, who will save them from the consequence of their plots? Do they assume they have deities who will help them? Let them know that Allah is exalted above whatever they associate with Him. 'Exalted is Allah above whatever they associate with Him' was explained: He cannot be associated with any attribute contrary to His status. The polytheists believed in His attributes but associated partners with Him, which negated His attributes; hence, it is stated that He is exalted above their polytheistic associations.

(And if they were to see a fragment from the sky falling, they would say, '[It is merely] a junction of clouds') (44)
Those who plot cannot be convinced:
This comforts the Prophet: since their objections are merely a plot, you cannot convince them. Even if they saw the punishment falling on them, they would regard it as a junction of clouds, assuming it is a rain cloud bringing rain. This points to the historical account of Thamud: when they saw the approaching punishment, they said: (This is a cloud bringing us rain) (Al-Ahqaf, 46:24). The meaning is: similarly, these people will believe only when the punishment destroys them, but believing at that moment is of no benefit. A caller should try to clear objections as long as the listener is sincere; but once it is clear that his objections are merely a plot to flee from reality, he should leave him to Allah.

(So leave them until they meet their Day in which they will be struck dumb—the Day their plan will not avail them at all, nor will they be helped) (45-46)
It is stated: since they are plotting, do not worry about them. Leave them until they face the Day when they will be struck dumb by the Trumpet, described in Surah Al-Hajj as: (And you will see the people as if they were intoxicated, while they are not intoxicated; but the punishment of Allah is severe) (22:2). (the Day their plan will not avail them) - on that Day, neither their plans nor their helpers and assumed partners will save them, but everyone will face Allah, Who will lay open their record.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 45 and 46 was combined in the original text.]

(And indeed, for those who have wronged is a punishment before that, but most of them do not know) (47)
Reference to the worldly punishment:
That is: besides the punishment of the Hereafter, those who wrong will face a punishment before that, but most of them do not know. This refers to the punishment that, according to the divine law, has always befallen those who rejected the Messenger, and which befell the Quraysh during the Prophet's life.

(And be patient, [O Muhammad], for the decision of your Lord, for indeed, you are in Our eyes. And exalt [Him] with praise of your Lord when you arise—and of the night exalt Him and after [the setting of] the stars) (48-49)
Instruction of patience and waiting to the Prophet:
In a highly affectionate manner, the Prophet is instructed to remain patient and wait: wait with patience and firmness for the decision of your Lord. (for indeed, you are in Our eyes) is a highly comforting phrase, meaning: you are in Our eyes (Our protection) at all times; let them plot, they cannot harm you.
The remedy of patience: (And exalt [Him] with praise of your Lord when you arise) - to attain patience, perform prayer, especially the night prayer (Tahajjud). (when you arise) (heen taqoom) was explained in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verses 217-218: (And rely upon the Exalted in Might, the Merciful, Who sees you when you arise). Similarly, (and of the night exalt Him and after [the setting] of the stars) appeared in Surah Qaf, verses 39-40; refer to those verses for details. We reproduce the verses of Surah Qaf:
(So be patient over what they say and exalt [Allah] with praise of your Lord before the rising of the sun and before its setting, and of the night exalt Him and after [the setting of] the stars).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 48 and 49 was combined in the original text.]
